title: Want to Think Better? Don't Ask AI for Help Right Away
summary: Have you ever asked a chatbot to help with homework? A new study says it might be better to wait before you do. Researchers had 393 people pretend to be city council members and decide on a water pollution problem. Some could use an AI chatbot right away, some could use it later, and some couldn't use it at all. They also gave people either plenty of time (30 minutes) or very little time (10 minutes). The results were surprising. People who had enough time and used the chatbot later — after thinking on their own first — wrote the best essays. They included more good arguments and looked at the problem from more angles. But people who never used the chatbot remembered the facts best. When time was tight, using the chatbot early helped the most. So what's the lesson? If you want to really learn and think deeply, try to work on a problem by yourself before asking AI. That way, you build your own understanding first. Then the chatbot can help you see things you missed. But if you're in a hurry, AI can give you a quick boost — just know that you might miss out on some of your own good ideas. The study's author, Mina Lee, says we all need to get smarter about when and how we use AI. It's like a tool: knowing when to pick it up makes all the difference.
why_it_matters: Knowing when to use AI can help you learn better in school and not rely too much on chatbots for answers, which might make your own brain lazy.

keywords.explanation: A computer program that can have a conversation with you.
Short for artificial intelligence, the technology that powers chatbots.
People who study things carefully to learn new facts.
People elected to make decisions for a city.
An issue where water becomes dirty and unsafe.
Reasons given to support an idea.

questions.question: What did the study ask people to pretend to be?
When did people write the best essays?
options: When they used the chatbot right away
When they used it later after thinking on their own
questions.correct_answer: When they used it later after thinking on their own

questions.question: Who remembered the facts best?
options: People who used the chatbot early
People who used the chatbot later
People who never used the chatbot
questions.correct_answer: People who never used the chatbot

questions.question: What did the study suggest if you’re in a hurry and need a quick boost?
options: Use AI after thinking a long time

questions.question: What is a lesson from the study?
options: AI always gives the best answers
It's better to think on your own first before using AI
questions.correct_answer: It's better to think on your own first before using AI

background_read: AI chatbots like ChatGPT became very popular in 2022 and can answer almost any question in seconds.
Mina Lee (a researcher at an American university) led this study to understand how AI affects learning.
Earlier studies have shown that if you try to solve a problem first, your brain remembers better because it works harder.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers led by Mina Lee and 393 people in an experiment.
WHAT: They tested whether using an AI chatbot early or later helps people think better and remember facts.
WHERE: In an online study where participants pretended to be city council members.
WHY: To learn the best way to use AI for learning without hurting our own thinking skills.

perspectives.description: AI can be a great helper to find new ideas and fill gaps after you've tried on your own.
If you always ask AI first, you may not develop your own problem-solving skills and might miss out on creative ideas.
The study shows that the timing of using AI matters, so we need to be smart about when we pick up this tool.
